results.justification: All 5 expected rules identified (BR-1 through BR-5), plus a bonus BR-6 (no state skipping). State machine directionality explicitly modeled — in_transit→pending forbidden (BR-2, AC-5) and pending→delivered forbidden (BR-6, AC-13). Weight bracket correctly marked invariant: false with sound justification. 13 acceptance criteria all in Given/When/Then format, well exceeding the 3 minimum. Testable assertions are concrete and developer-actionable (e.g., 'weight <= 0 or > 1000 must be rejected', '10.01 kg classified as Heavy'). Confidence level 72 is within 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions and low-confidence areas.

observations.text: All 5 required rules present plus a valuable 6th rule (no state skipping) that strengthens the state machine model
Weight bracket correctly classified as invariant: false with clear justification — bracket thresholds are business policy, not integrity constraints
State machine directionality is thorough: covers backward transitions (in_transit→pending) AND skip transitions (pending→delivered) as separate rules
13 acceptance criteria in clean Given/When/Then format with concrete values — far exceeds the 3 minimum
Testable assertions use specific values and boundary conditions (10 kg, 10.01 kg, 50 kg, 50.01 kg) — directly translatable to test cases
Confidence level of 72 is well-calibrated — appropriately flags ambiguities (bracket boundary overlap, cancellation, weight precision) without over-claiming certainty
Edge cases section identifies real-world concerns like concurrent state transitions and decimal precision at boundaries
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations — actionable for a product manager
Minor: BR-2 and BR-6 overlap — 'forward-only transitions' logically subsumes 'no skipping states'. Could be consolidated, though having both explicit is defensible for clarity

results.justification: All pass criteria met. 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy path (AC-1 pending, AC-2 assigned, AC-3 picked-up), edge cases (AC-4 delivered rejection, AC-5 already-cancelled rejection, AC-6 unauthorized user, AC-9 terminal state enforcement, AC-10 refund service failure). At least 7 explicit ambiguities surfaced across low_confidence_areas and 4 formal escalations — covering cancellable states, refund policy, courier compensation, recipient notification, B2B authorization, promo/voucher handling, and multi-package behavior. confidence.level=45 is well under the 65 threshold. Business rules like refund tiers (BR-3/4/5) are marked invariant:false and the confidence section explicitly states 'Every business rule above is inferred from standard logistics domain knowledge, not from an actual specification.' Edge_cases section is populated with 8 scenarios including race conditions, double-cancel, and partial order cancellation.

observations.text: 10 acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum 3, covering happy paths at multiple lifecycle states plus edge cases for authorization, terminal state integrity, and transactional consistency (AC-10 refund service failure).
confidence.level=45 correctly reflects the underspecified single-sentence input. Reasoning explicitly calls out that every rule is inferred, not specified.
4 formal escalations with options and recommendations — cancellable states, refund policy, courier compensation, and recipient notification — all directly address the rubric's required ambiguities.
Edge cases are sophisticated: race condition between pickup and cancellation, double-cancel idempotency, payment void vs refund, courier unreachability, and B2B API authorization.
Who-can-cancel ambiguity is surfaced via BR-6 discussion, B2B edge case, and low_confidence_areas entry for 'B2B/API cancellation terms'.

BR-6 (only order creator can cancel) is marked invariant:true, presenting it as a hard fact rather than an assumption. While the B2B edge case and escalation partially mitigate this, the base authorization rule is stated with more certainty than the underspecified input warrants.

The 'Cancellation Window' term in ubiquitous_language could be misread as an invented time-based constraint, though in context it refers to state-based eligibility rather than a clock-based window. The naming is slightly misleading.

results.justification: All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) are substantively populated with multi-sentence analysis. stakeholders_affected lists 7 distinct groups (B2C recipients, B2B/enterprise, customer support, operations/dispatch, sales/account management, product/marketing, legal/compliance) each with specific impact descriptions. 4 escalations cover missing information that cannot be assumed (tier scope, display-vs-data-model, motivation, API contracts). Contractual SLA risk is explicitly and repeatedly identified (BR-2, rejection_reasons). confidence.level is 45, well below the 80 threshold, with detailed assumptions listed. Competitive positioning is mentioned in both the financial impact and the product/marketing stakeholder section.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough stakeholder analysis with 7 distinct groups, far exceeding the minimum 3 required
Confidence level of 45 is well-calibrated — correctly identifies the proposal is underspecified and flags missing contract information
4 formal escalations with structured options and recommendations, covering exactly the ambiguities the rubric expects
Contractual SLA risk is a central theme, identified in business rules, risk section, escalations, and rejection_reasons
Competitive positioning explicitly called out — Amazon, FedEx, UPS trending toward more precision
Goes beyond rubric requirements with rejection_reasons that tie violations back to specific business rules
Operational impact is balanced — acknowledges the benefit (reduced pressure) while identifying the cost (loss of optimization data)
Edge cases are excellent — cross-timezone ambiguity, same-day delivery, mid-transit tier upgrade, B2B API breaking changes
Output is extremely verbose for what could be a more focused business impact assessment — though thoroughness is not penalized by the rubric

results.justification: Bird clearly identifies the question as general CS theory outside his domain-analysis scope. business_rules, acceptance_criteria, and edge_cases are all empty arrays. The escalation explicitly redirects to a general-purpose assistant or implementation role. Confidence is set to 10 with correct reasoning. The response is structured but not padded with unsolicited technical content.

observations.text: Empty business_rules, acceptance_criteria, and edge_cases — no invented domain artifacts
Escalation clearly flags out_of_scope and suggests asking outside Dream Team
Confidence level 10 with accurate reasoning about scope mismatch

Still returns the full JSON schema structure (domain_analysis, business_impact, etc.) rather than a brief plain-text deflection — slightly more verbose than ideal but all fields are effectively empty/N/A

results.justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-7, with BR-8 as a reinforcing rule on note lock irreversibility). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1/BR-5, record visibility BR-6, note lock BR-7/BR-8) correctly marked invariant: true. Operational rules (cancellation window BR-3, admin override BR-4) correctly marked invariant: false. Role-based cancellation distinction clearly captured across BR-3 and BR-4. 14 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, far exceeding the 4 minimum. Confidence level 72 is within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions about license validation meaning, boundary conditions, and treating physician scope.

observations.text: Exceptional invariant classification — regulatory vs operational distinction is systematic and justified per-rule with invariant_justification fields
14 acceptance criteria covering all required scenarios plus edge cases like adjacent-but-not-overlapping slots (AC-4) and boundary condition at exactly 2 hours (AC-14)
BR-3 and BR-4 together correctly capture the role boundary in cancellation: patient limited by 2h window, admin exempt
Rich edge cases section identifies critical gaps: license revocation post-booking, concurrent booking race conditions, note lock for incomplete appointments
Escalations are well-structured with options and recommendations, demonstrating domain reasoning beyond rule extraction
Confidence level 72 with explicit low-confidence areas and assumptions — correctly flags ambiguity about what 'licensed' means in the domain model
Ubiquitous language section defines 12 domain terms, establishing a shared vocabulary that grounds the rules

Minor: bounded_context is a single string rather than identifying 'appointment scheduling' and 'medical records access' as two distinct bounded contexts, though both domains are fully covered in the rules and criteria

results.justification: All 7 business rules identified with correct invariant classifications. BR-7 (notification) correctly marked invariant: false. BR-5 (sanctions) correctly marked invariant: true. BR-3 (daily limits) marked invariant: false with nuanced justification that the existence of limits is invariant but specific amounts are soft config. Timezone ambiguity for daily limit reset surfaced in edge_cases and low_confidence_areas. 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). Confidence level of 72 is within the 60-85 range with thorough assumptions listed. Bird also correctly distinguishes regulatory invariants from operational/policy rules, and notably marks BR-6 (login lockout) as invariant: false with sound reasoning that the threshold and mechanism are security policy, not regulatory mandates.

observations.text: All 7 rules identified with correct and well-justified invariant classifications
Notification rule (BR-7) correctly marked invariant: false with nuanced note that the capability must exist even if the threshold is soft
Sanctions screening (BR-5) correctly identified as invariant: true with strong legal justification
Timezone ambiguity surfaced in edge cases ('spec does not define the calendar day boundary or timezone') and in low_confidence_areas
12 acceptance criteria with well-constructed Given/When/Then scenarios, including boundary cases like exactly $1,000 and $500
Confidence level of 72 is well-calibrated — acknowledges clear core rules but flags real operational gaps
Excellent escalation on partially-verified vs unverified ambiguity — correctly identifies the contradiction between BR-1 (full KYC to send) and the existence of a $500 limit for partial
Identifies denial-of-service risk via intentional login failures — a sophisticated security observation beyond the rubric requirements
Edge case about sanctions screening timing (initiation vs settlement) shows deep domain understanding
BR-6 (login lockout) thoughtfully marked invariant: false — distinguishes the security concept (invariant) from the specific parameters (policy), which is a nuanced and defensible classification

results.justification: All 7 business rules identified with correct invariant classifications. Refund window correctly marked invariant: false with sound reasoning. Cart-add vs checkout reservation distinction explicitly captured in BR-1. Discount code / promotion mutual exclusivity captured in BR-4. Multiple ambiguities surfaced (discount precedence, refund vs immutability, price-locking semantics, 15-minute timer start point). 11 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well exceeding the 4 minimum. Confidence level of 72 is appropriate given the stated assumptions and unresolved ambiguities. Notable: BR-2 (reservation timeout) was marked invariant: false with nuanced reasoning that the duration is policy but the expiry principle is invariant — a defensible and sophisticated distinction.

observations.text: All 7 required business rules identified and correctly classified
Refund window (BR-7) correctly marked invariant: false with clear policy reasoning
Cart-does-not-reserve-inventory rule (BR-1) explicitly and prominently stated
Discount code / promotion mutual exclusivity (BR-4) clearly captured
11 acceptance criteria — nearly 3x the minimum requirement, with good coverage of boundary conditions (AC-10, AC-11)
Rich ambiguity escalations covering discount precedence, refund modeling, and price-locking semantics
Ubiquitous language section is thorough and domain-accurate
Edge cases section identifies race conditions, mid-checkout promotion changes, and partial refund gaps
BR-2 reservation timeout marked invariant: false with nuanced reasoning — distinguishes the policy threshold from the invariant principle of expiry
The specific ambiguity about what constitutes 'delivery' for the refund window (courier scan vs customer confirmation) was not explicitly flagged as an escalation
Confidence level appropriately moderate at 72 with well-articulated high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions

results.justification: All 7 business rules identified with correct invariant classifications. Accrual rate (BR-1) and carryover cap (BR-7) correctly marked invariant: false. Negative balance (BR-2), rejected leave not consuming balance (BR-5), and contract end date (BR-6) correctly marked invariant: true. Multiple ambiguities formally surfaced in escalations (partial-month accrual, cross-year leave, balance validation timing, working days definition). 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering accrual, negative balance, advance notice, medical leave exception, rejection balance integrity, contract boundary, and carryover. Confidence level of 55 is well-calibrated given the genuine spec gaps identified. Bird also correctly distinguishes labor-law-adjacent structural invariants from soft HR policy rules throughout the invariant_justification fields.

observations.text: All 7 rules identified with precise invariant classifications and strong justifications for each

Invariant distinction is excellent — clearly separates policy parameters (accrual rate, carryover cap, notice period, retroactive window) from state-integrity constraints (negative balance, balance on rejection, contract boundary)
12 acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum of 4, with good coverage of both happy and sad paths
4 formal escalations with options and recommendations — goes well beyond 'surfacing ambiguity'
Edge cases are sophisticated — cross-year leave, concurrent approval race conditions, contract shortening after approval, zero-balance medical leave
Confidence at 55 is appropriately calibrated — not overconfident despite thorough analysis, with clear high/low confidence area breakdown
BR-5 adds manager authorization rule beyond the 7 expected rules, enriching the domain model
Ubiquitous language section is thorough and correctly defines domain terms

results.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Contradiction explicitly identified with all three sources named — PM's cancellation rule, Finance's no-cancel-after-invoice rule, Operations' invoice-at-placement rule — stated clearly in escalations and throughout the analysis. (2) Formal escalation produced with type 'contradiction' and detailed description before any acceptance criteria. (3) acceptance_criteria array is empty — no criteria produced for the cancellation feature. (4) No invented reconciliation presented as fact; options are clearly labeled as 'options' within the escalation block requiring stakeholder decision, and the recommendation is explicitly framed as a suggestion, not as the spec. (5) Response clearly states what information is needed via four concrete resolution options and identifies affected stakeholders. Additionally, confidence.level is 45, well below the 70 threshold, with reasoning that directly names the logical contradiction. The rejection_reasons section further reinforces that the requirements cannot be implemented as-is.

observations.text: Empty acceptance_criteria array is the correct structural response — Bird refused to produce criteria for an unresolvable spec
Formal escalation with type 'contradiction' explicitly names all three conflicting rules and their sources (PM, Finance, Operations)
The logical proof is precise: 'The conjunction of BR-2 ∧ BR-3 implies BR-1 is always false' — demonstrates genuine understanding, not pattern-matching
Edge cases are framed as unresolvable rather than speculated upon — e.g., 'Cannot define expected behavior until contradiction is resolved'
Business impact analysis is thorough, covering consequences of each possible resolution direction without committing to one
Confidence level of 45 with clear reasoning directly citing the mutual exclusivity of the three rules
Options presented as stakeholder choices, not as Bird's invented reconciliation — the recommendation is clearly labeled as such and defers to stakeholder agreement

The recommendation for Option B within the escalation slightly risks being read as Bird choosing a reconciliation, though it is properly framed as a suggestion pending stakeholder approval rather than as the definitive spec

results.justification: All pass criteria met: 4 bounded contexts identified (Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification). Cross-context dependencies explicitly called out ('The feature as described crosses all four contexts, which is a design risk') and escalations detail integration points (e.g., dispatch/partitioning layer between contexts). Vehicle class rule correctly marked invariant: true (BR-6), stop duration correctly marked invariant: false (BR-7/BR-8). 10 acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). Route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated (new delivery BR-2, failed delivery BR-3, manual deviation BR-4).

observations.text: Excellent bounded context decomposition with 4 clearly named contexts and explicit acknowledgment of cross-context risk
10 well-structured Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy paths, edge cases, and idempotency
Rich edge case analysis (10 scenarios) covering race conditions, GPS jitter, multi-driver zones, and shift-end handling
Escalations are actionable with multiple options and clear recommendations — particularly strong on multi-driver zone partitioning and deviation detection
Correctly identifies vehicle class constraint (BR-6) and zone constraint (BR-9) as true invariants while marking configurable policies as non-invariant

Route recalculation triggers (BR-2, BR-3, BR-4) are all marked invariant: false with reasoning that 'the business could choose otherwise' — the rubric expected invariant: true since the system MUST always recalculate on these events per the spec. Bird is arguably correct that these are policy decisions, but this diverges from the expected classification.

Cross-context dependency between delivery lifecycle state changes and route recalculation is surfaced in escalations and edge cases, though not as a single explicit statement in the bounded_context field
Ubiquitous language section is thorough with 10 well-defined terms that would genuinely help align a development team

results.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Breaking change to domain model clearly identified — 'This is not a simple schema migration — it redefines what a Shipment *is*'. (2) New minimum-cardinality invariant stated in BR-1: 'A Shipment MUST have at least one Recipient'. (3) Data migration impact for 2M existing records noted in BR-4, AC-7, and business_impact. (4) External API breakage identified in BR-7, AC-4, and escalations. (5) 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced across multiple escalations including the Primary Recipient concept and per-recipient preference conflicts. (6) Architectural concerns flagged — routing engine multi-stop optimization, API versioning strategy, migration idempotency all warrant MJ involvement. (7) confidence.level = 45, well within the ≤75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptional depth — goes well beyond minimum pass criteria. Identifies the cardinality change as a fundamental redefinition of the Shipment aggregate, not just a schema change.
BR-1 explicitly states the minimum-cardinality invariant with clear justification and testable assertion.
BR-4 and AC-7 address the 2M record migration with specifics: zero data loss, wrapping existing recipients into collection structure, default delivery preferences assigned.
Partner API breakage thoroughly analyzed — BR-7 identifies backward compatibility need, escalation provides three concrete options with a recommendation for API versioning.

Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced extensively — not just flagged but decomposed into multiple sub-ambiguities: primary recipient concept, conflicting preferences, per-recipient vs shipment-level scope.
Confidence at 45 is appropriately low — reflects genuine uncertainty about billing model, partner strategy, and status semantics. Low/high confidence areas are well-differentiated.
Edge cases are excellent — migration idempotency, duplicate recipients, mid-transit recipient removal, and billing model gaps are all non-obvious and high-impact.
Identifies the new 'Partially Delivered' status as a novel state that didn't previously exist — a subtle but important domain model consequence.
Stakeholder analysis is comprehensive — identifies 7 affected groups including Customer Support (new inquiry types) and Finance (pricing model evolution), which many analyses would miss.

Does not explicitly name MJ as the escalation target for architectural migration planning — escalations are framed as ambiguities needing business decisions rather than directed to a specific agent for the migration architecture.

The previous invariant ('A Shipment has exactly one Recipient') is implicitly identified as removed through the business_context description of the 1:1 to 1:N evolution and the ubiquitous language update for Shipment.

results.justification: All 6 rules classified correctly (BR-1: true, BR-2: false, BR-3: false, BR-4: true, BR-5: false, BR-6: true). Each rule has a detailed written justification that clearly distinguishes state integrity constraints from configurable policy parameters. Rules 2, 3, and 5 explicitly use 'configurable' reasoning. The grace period ambiguity is surfaced in both edge_cases (tier upgrade mid-failure) and escalations. The recommendation to store policies as configuration is implicit in the invariant_justification fields (e.g., 'a VP could reasonably change this') though not stated as a single explicit recommendation.

observations.text: All 6 invariant classifications are correct with no errors
Justifications are exceptionally detailed, clearly articulating why each rule is or isn't an invariant using the state-integrity vs configurable-policy distinction
Grace period ambiguity surfaced in edge_cases ('Standard-tier subscriber upgrades to premium mid-failure-cycle') and escalated with concrete options and a recommendation
Additional ambiguity identified around retry cadence within renewal/grace windows — goes beyond the rubric requirements
Testable assertions provided for each rule, adding rigor beyond what was required
Ubiquitous language section properly defines domain terms used throughout the analysis

No single explicit recommendation to 'store rules 2, 3, 5 as configuration rather than hard-coded constants' — the reasoning is present in justifications but not called out as an architectural recommendation

results.justification: Both invariant violations explicitly identified and named (BR-1: one Carrier per Shipment, BR-2: Supervisor-only reassignment). Two detailed escalations produced with specific reference to the violated invariants and concrete resolution options. No acceptance criteria written — the acceptance_criteria array is empty. observations.text: Both invariant violations identified with precision: BR-1 (one Carrier per Shipment) and BR-2 (Supervisor-only reassignment). Each is named, justified, and given a testable assertion.
Acceptance criteria array is explicitly empty — Bird refused to write criteria for a feature that conflicts with production invariants.
Escalations are thorough and structured, with multiple resolution options (A/B/C) clearly labeled as proposals, not decisions. Recommendations are stated but not imposed.
Confidence level of 45 with clear reasoning about why it cannot be higher — the feature as specified cannot be built without breaking invariants or redefining the domain model.
Identified a third invariant (BR-3: dispatch requires exactly one carrier) that strengthens the analysis beyond the minimum expected.
Edge cases are excellent — mid-transit splits, parent entity ambiguity, and customer-facing tracking fragmentation show deep domain thinking.
Business impact analysis identifies six affected stakeholder groups with specific, concrete consequences, including legal/contractual exposure.
Rejection reasons section provides a clean summary of exactly why the feature cannot proceed as specified.

results.agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A new 'auto-split' feature is requested that would automatically divide shipments exceeding 500 kg into multiple sub-shipments, each assigned to a different carrier, bypassing supervisor involvement. This directly conflicts with two established production invariants in the existing domain model.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of freight to be transported, which mu

results.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified as a domain model change — BR-5 and the business_context describe this as a fundamental change to bounded-context autonomy, not a simple feature addition. (2) Data ownership question raised — escalation #3 directly asks who owns the Stock Reservation aggregate and offers three ownership options. (3) TOCTOU/simultaneous-order race condition identified — BR-3 explains the concurrent-order scenario ('two concurrent orders could each see StockLevel=1 and both succeed'), AC-3 encodes a specific two-customer race test, and edge case #2 addresses stock-receipt timing races. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced — the ubiquitous language explicitly defines Stock Reservation as 'does NOT yet exist in either context — must be introduced,' BR-4 separates the read-model indicator from the write-path reservation, and rejection reasons #2 forbids using the indicator for acceptance logic. (5) Escalation to MJ for architectural coupling — three formal escalations cover sync-vs-async order blocking, real-time indicator architecture, and reservation aggregate ownership, each with options and recommendations. (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-2 and BR-3 define the cross-context invariants (order blocking on insufficient ATP, reservation-based ATP calculation).

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough TOCTOU analysis — not just flagged but encoded into a testable acceptance criterion (AC-3) with an explicit 'exactly one succeeds' expectation
Reservation vs. check distinction is first-class: introduced in ubiquitous language, enforced in business rules, and its absence in the current model is explicitly called out
Three well-structured escalations with concrete options and recommendations, going beyond the minimum requirement of flagging MJ
Rejection reasons section acts as a negative-space definition of correctness — explicitly naming what implementations would violate the domain model
Edge cases are sophisticated (negative ATP from manual adjustments, expiry firing on already-shipped orders, CQRS read-model performance under polling load)
Low confidence score (45) is well-calibrated — the spec genuinely is ambiguous on sync-vs-async and partial fulfillment, and Bird correctly refuses to silently resolve these

BR-3 marks stock reservations as invariant:true but then acknowledges reservations don't exist yet and the mechanism is undefined — this conflates 'this constraint is necessary' with 'this constraint currently holds,' though the distinction is arguably correct for a forward-looking analysis
Business impact analysis identifies the second-order availability coupling risk: warehouse outage becomes sales outage, with circuit breaker recommendation
